next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

At 8:11, the production board in MiraeWave's Building C already looked like a threat. First story conference in nineteen minutes. Second at ten with advertisers. Noon with Standards. Two with Director Kwon. Four with Haneul Entertainment and Ji-eun's manager. Final script lock by midnight if everyone lied about how long things took. She had read the same rundown in her kitchen at dawn, then walked into rain hard enough to pull fryer oil and wet cardboard into the curb while the Han pushed its sweet-rot breath upriver. Inside Building C, reheated coffee and monitor ozone clung to the hallway. Ina wiped rain from her glasses with the hem of her blouse, saw the smear she made, and wiped again. Her left wrist tugged where the old burn scar tightened in damp weather.

The writers' room looked like a school classroom designed by people who hated windows. The walls were paper-thin, and marker fumes hung in the heat from a fan that turned without moving any air. Three whiteboards divided into columns: ROMANCE ARC, SPONSOR TOUCHPOINTS, SENSITIVE CONTENT. Under SENSITIVE CONTENT, somebody had written in red, all caps: NO FLAGS. NO BORDER MAPS. NO SPEECHES.

Executive producer Nam So-young was already there, barefoot under the table, shoes lined up neatly beside her chair because her left heel blistered if she sat in pumps for more than an hour. She rolled a dried persimmon between her fingers like a prayer bead, then bit it in half without looking up from the rating sheet.

"You're late."

"I'm two minutes early," Ina said, hanging her wet tote on the back of her chair....
open_hooks_to_carry: Quiet dinner conversation includes non-strategic memory and humor before conflict rises.
Dohyun admits Eun-sook believed stories could move people more than policy did.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...MPTION`.

The app refreshed.

`Contractor liability activated.`

`Non-payment may trigger recovery actions against registered guarantor-linked assets.`

The sedan turned onto the bridge toward Mapo. Water below was flat pewter under dawn cloud. Ina watched her reflection in the window drift over the river and superimpose on cranes and apartment blocks and construction hoardings promising luxury towers to people who did not need to count coins before buying transit fare.

She pressed the burn scar on her left wrist with her thumb until the old skin whitened.

By seven-thirty she had made two entries on two different ledgers: one payment that kept her father out of immediate collections, and one transfer that kept Ji-eun from carrying today's punishment alone.

The mechanism was plain. If Ina kept delivering, her lines would move through actors, agencies, edits, and USB copies she could not track. If one of those lines tripped a sweep, the person pulled into a windowless room would not be Kwon, and it would not be the lawyers who wrote the clauses.

There was no clean version left, only distribution of damage.

That was the story she built before sunrise, and it did not forgive her.

By the time the car stopped outside her building, she had revised it three times and believed none of the revisions fully. She carried the contract upstairs anyway.
By late afternoon she had countersigned three intake addenda, bought eggs she could not afford without checking her balance twice, and headed to her father's apartment when Hyejin's second text replaced a question with an instruction.
state_deltas_required: Decide whether to continue after initial recruitment.
Ina stays in the operation but sets an internal red line against civilian harm.
